comments.title: Art of Memory and Renaissance Magic
comments.content:
Hey Mr. Greer, first time asking a question -- actually I'm pretty new to this whole magic business, having been a lifelong skeptic who has now seen one too many metaphysical phenomena to be able to deny it any longer. In any case, I'm pretty interested in the Art of Memory and Renaissance Magic, especially after having read Couliano's Eros and Magic in the Renaissance. If it isn't too much of an ask, I'd like to further sources on both subjects, but on a more practical sense rather than something like Couliano's intelectual analysis.

That aside, I'd just like to thank you for your work. It's mighty helpful to have someone writing in a down-to-earth way about magic topics, especially for beginners like me.

replies.title: Re: Art of Memory and Renaissance Magic
replies.content: The best source on the art of memory is, sensibly enough, The Art of Memory by Frances Yates; it's a history, but it gives the details of the method clearly enough that it's easy for a beginner to pick it up. Renaissance magic -- that's a more difficult question because there's so much of it. Imagine asking someone for a book that would introduce them to science. Renaissance magic is like that -- it includes many different methods, practices, fields of work, and disciplines. Frances Yates' The Occult Philosophy in the Elizabethan Age is a decent overview but not practically oriented; Cornelius Agrippa's Three Books of Occult Philosophy is practically oriented but very difficult for the modern student to make sense of. Is there a particular kind of Renaissance magic you'd like to start with?

replies.title: Re: Art of Memory and Renaissance Magic
replies.content: Thanks for the answer! As to what kind of Renaissance magic, I'll admit I didn't think that far, but I did get very interested in the whole idea of the Pneuma as Couliano described it. Maybe that's still too vague?

replies.title: Re: Art of Memory and Renaissance Magic
replies.content: Not vague so much as general -- every form of Renaissance magic works with pneuma, just as every form of science works with matter. Do you want to work wih pneuma that's already descended into material things?  That's natural magic. Do you want to work with pneuma that cascades down to the earth from the planets and stars?  That's astrological magic. Do you want to work with pneuma that's embodied in spiritual beings?  That's spiritual magic. Those are the three great divisions, and there are many subsets within each of them.

replies.title: Re: Art of Memory and Renaissance Magic
replies.content: Man, I suppose I really don't know anything. If I'm gonna be honest all sound pretty interesting, but natural magic piqued my interest the most.

replies.title: Re: Art of Memory and Renaissance Magic
replies.content:
One of the downsides of Couliano's book is that he didn't cover the basics.

In terms of natural magic, if you don't mind a plug, my book The Encyclopedia of Natural Magic is based squarely on medieval and Renaissance sources and has been very popular as an introductory text since it first came out more than 20 years ago.

comments.content:
Good Evening,

If you've been dealing with the cold weather that's hit much of the US, I hope it has gone well, and if you're getting some of the warmer weather that we're looking for where I am, I hope it's welcome!

To Share: For those who have been following along, I've (finally) gotten to the end of Maria Kvilhaug's The Seed of Yggdrasill, and share my thoughts on the last chapter here: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_the_seed_of_yggdrasill_12_the_one_and_the_many_and_epilogue

I might also put together a rundown of best and worst things about the book as a whole as a "one stop shop" post now that I've thought my way through the whole thing.

To Ask: In readying my question, I realized I had made a mistake in what I was going to ask about, so two related questions, if I may:

1) I did a pathworking for Fearn, but I screwed up and began in Alban Eiler, rather than Imbolc. Other than doing it over again, the right way, anything I should keep in mind about the experience and the follow-up meditations I did on it? Are they in any way "suspect" or possibly messing up solidifying the symbolic framework of the Wheel of Life and its paths?

2) While along the path, I encountered something that my guide told me I wasn't yet ready for. When meditation and divination suggest I am ready to go back to that thing, should I a) scry Fearn with the intention of going back to the same astral "place", b) do a proper pathworking of Fearn from Imbolc to Belteinne, c) recreate my screwed up pathworking where I began in Alban Eiler, opened a portal to Fearn, and ended in Belteinne, so as to go back to the "same" astral territory, or d) something else?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Review of Last Chapter of Seed of Yggdrasill and Returning to a "Place" from a Pathworking
replies.content:
Jeff, it's a Mediterranean climate, named for that region and also found in parts of coastal Australia and South Africa.

Advantages: usually mild conditions for easy comfort year round (though in places air conditioning isn't a luxury), only very rare extreme storms, long growing season, usually no snow below easily reached mountains, no road salt so vehicles and roads last a long time, most precipitation is in winter so dry summers, seldom issues with mold.

Disadvantages: extremely subtle changes of season, not much for fall colors, low total precipitation for the year can make big fires a risk (with big mudslides from following rains), usually no snow below mountains, trapped hot summer air can get stagnant, flash floods a risk in dry channels, grass and gardens and bigger trees can require irrigation, agricultural and urban scale water supply can require enormous public infrastructure to redistribute water.

If I was rich, I'd enjoy cooler summers into fall colors somewhere farther north, then return here before winter gets inconvenient. Pasadena's Rose Parade for example was founded by midwesterners with winter getaway homes here.

In L.A. the weather report answers the question, "In what way will we be comfortable today? T-shirt or some more layers?"
In the midwest the weather report answers the question, "In what way is the sky trying to kill us today, so we can defend ourselves?"

If you don't mind a comedy where the plot is one third sex farce, Steve Martin's movie "L.A. Story" has the best depiction of southern California weather ever put on film. And a lot of very funny parody along the way. You and your wife might recognize some of his exaggerations from your time in San Diego.

- Christopher

comments.title: MOE Master Attunement; Blessings; "Seeing without eyes"
comments.content:
Good evening/morning to JMG and everybody else! :-)


1. Reminder: MOE Master Attunement on Sunday, February 4th, at 6pm German time

More info and room for questions: https://thehiddenthings.com/moe-master-attunement-on-sunday-february-4th-2024


2. I offer weekly blessings. These are open to everybody, i.e. not only for MOE practitioners:

https://thehiddenthings.com/categories/weekly-blessings

(I want to practice my blessing skills, so you'd do me a favour in signing up... :-) )


3. And finally, a question of the type "how does this work?":

Somebody recently told me about "seeing without eyes" ("Sehen ohne Auge"), which he and his kids learned through a workshop. They started with some "intuition training", and then learned to "see" things with their eyes covered. He said his kids learned it within a few hours, while he is still practicing. Once somebody has developed that faculty, they can recognize objects, distinguish colours and, with some practice, read passages from physical books or random-generated numbers from a computer screen (all with their eyes closed or covered), and he has personally seen examples of this from some people.

I have no reason to doubt this guy's account, but I've been wondering how that works.

My initial guess was something on the astral level (i.e.. "connecting" to the object or book in question on the astral, and determining the properties/"reading" the text this way).

But apparently, the people who do this don't "see" the things in their mind's eye. Instead, there seems to be a small "vision field" which opens up (usually circular, and for most people in the lower part of their "physical" vision field"), where they can "see" these things as if they'd see them through their physical eyes. With training, this vision field can widen. (I've found videos of kids demonstrating reading with this method, and they actually moved their head in unusual angles to get the objects into the right place in order to see them, which seems to confirm this account.)

I.e. there is a weird mixture of occult things and physical influences going on - and now curious mind is wondering about the mechanism behind it... Since this person could only give me some generic talking points about the third eye and about connecting to the morphogenetic field:

Do you have any idea how this might work, or what might be going on there? Or any pointers to similar things I could compare that with?

Thanks! :-)

Milkyway

replies.title: Re: Magical Curriculum and Geomancy
replies.content:
1) This notion of "traditions" is overemphasized. If you read accounts of mages back in the Middle Ages and Renaissance, no two of them did exactly the same things, and most of them cobbled together some set of approaches that worked for them and called it good. If you want to work with divination, meditation, talismans, etc., study and practice those subjects and learn from your experience.

2) It was much used for that purpose back in the day. As for whether it's effective or not, that depends on the practitioner -- any given oracle will be wonderfully effective for some people, mediocre for others, and a complete flop for still others.

replies.title: Re: Capabilities
replies.content: Every human being has a capacity for magnificence in some field. It can be literally anything; I once knew a guy whose magnificence was as a short order cook. (People would line up around the block for his breaded deep-fried mushrooms.) Every soul has its own unique talents, but some lives will go into developing those talents and others will go into working off karma or doing other things. Thus every soul has an equal capacity for greatness, but no two souls have a capacity for greatness in exactly the same things, and in any given life some people will develop one or more of those capacities and others will not.

replies.title: Re: Capabilities
replies.content: That makes sense.  If somebody reaches the full extent of their capabilities is that the end of their trajectory as a human? To use George Patton as an example. It would seem he went as far as one could go as a  soldier. So would he spend the rest of his incarnations (if he had karma to burn off) as a nun working at a homeless shelter in Des Moines? Or if he had no karma to burn off would his soul go on to be an apprentice to Mars or a similar deity? -Croatoan

replies.title: Re: Capabilities
replies.content:
Patton's a fascinating case. He believed -- and he had the past life memories to back it up -- that he was incarnated over and over again as a warrior when there was some task that only a master warrior could take on. His incarnation as George Patton was for the purpose of defeating Nazi Germany, and once Hitler's regime fell, Patton promptly died. So he may have been a spirit of Mars in the first place, taking on human form only for special missions.

In terms of more general questions, it really depends. It's been suggested, for example, that the Baroque composer Antonio Vivaldi was reincarnated as Mozart. Vivaldi was a first-rate composer, one of the greatest, and of course so was Mozart; clearly that soul had a lot of music to write!

comments.title: Request for prayers/magic for wife going through labour
comments.content:
My wife is in the early part of labour right now.

The original due date for our child was supposed to be this Sunday but the doctor advised us to induce labour last Sunday as my wife seemed to have high blood pressure.

We were hesitant to induce in the first place, I actually called them up and said that we would rather not induce, they said we could come down for the checkup on Sunday and then make a decision, but right away when I wasn't around (after visiting hours) the doctor (who wasn't the one we spoke to over the phone) inserted the labour induction drug.

They already removed it, but it didn't really start active labour anyway. My wife's cervix is at 1cm dilated, some contractions have started according to the machine, but they're not really strong enough for my wife to notice.

At this point, my wife just doesn't feel good about it, we both really wanted to have a natural delivery if possible and they seem to be pushing towards a Caesarean section now.

As far as I can see, the baby's heart rate is normal, my wife, when she's relaxed, has a normal blood pressure level. I am not a doctor but I feel that there is no need for a Caesarean right now, and tomorrow I will ask for her to be discharged and for us to return after the due date if active labour hasn't started.

Anyway, I am doing my own practices and prayers, but would appreciate any positive energy directed our way for a healthy and smooth delivery.

Thank you!
Alvin from Singapore

comments.title: Can magically impulsed mushrooms help clean the Earth?
comments.content:

Looking online I also found out about mycoremediation, a branch of bioremediation that uses a type of fungi grown on toxic wastelands that has evolved increased "hyperaccumulation"(they manage to store the metals at the fruiting body instead of the roots) in order to clean the earth and water that has become polluted --usually with metals from the mining and oil industry-- by inoculating a strain of that fungi into other contaminated areas and then removing it with the waste.

It turns out this is not incredibly hard and is the cheapest in cleaning sites also. Paul Stamets shows some of this on Mycelium Running as well as other effects of mushrooms in contaminants.

comments.title: Divination and the Ordinary
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Question 1: Do you know of a good source for guidelines on choosing topics for divination? Including your books, if any pertain.

To clarify a bit, some habits I've picked up are to avoid doing "big" divinations on major people and choices in my life but instead to constrain the topic and get a view of larger matters by way of many small divinations. So, for example, I won't do a reading on the person "Bob," but rather I'll divine on "my meeting with Bob tomorrow" and "what I'm doing with Bob this week." Rather than divining on a big life plan I divine on smaller events related to it.

This means I can determine the veracity of a reading more quickly and it helps me avoid locking myself into one set of possibilities. I'm finding I can determine fairly well if a person or situation is good to be involved with or not once I've divined on a few of these related topics. What do you think of this approach and can you recommend any other guidelines on topic selection?

Question 2: Have you or any others noticed that when you carry out a successful magical working, something feels very ordinary and matter-of-fact about it? Something I've noticed is that when I achieve my exact intended result, there's a tendency for it to feel uneventful, like "of course that's what was going to happen." It's the exact opposite of the impression given by something like a mage casting a fireball in a fantasy story. Do other people perceive this or is it just me?

Thanks as always for these posts.

replies.title: Re: Divination and the Ordinary
replies.content:
To your question #2:

Yes, that's exactly how I always feel after a successful magical working. (However, I use magic very rarely, only when mundane measures prove insufficient and an inner voice tells me that magic is an appropriate response in this particular case.)

replies.title: Re: Divination and the Ordinary
replies.content:
Not JMG here. Some opinions for you to take or leave as you like.

1) "Keep the know-it-all out of the process, and ask a single, unambiguous, carefully crafted question. To develop competence at this, keep a journal, and keep looking back at the quality of your previous interpretations."
- Bradford Hatcher, "The Book of Changes"

Divination is openness to an answer from beyond ordinary consciousness, about questions that one finds important. So which questions do you find most important? You have been asking as though "what's central to this one specific facet of a nearby situation" is the most important kind of question. It seems to be working for you! Would you like to try using some of your divinations for other kinds of questions, and compare how that works for you?

2) Neville Goddard's work was full of this very idea, one of its foremost teachers. He constantly taught to focus on assuming the naturalness of the answer already received, a feeling or situation inevitable when success is already present. A friend's congratulations for one's already started new job, the wedding ring worn in the already happy new marriage. Or, merely the feeling itself, such as, "isn't it wonderful!"

Here's how he put it.

"To rise in consciousness to the naturalness of the thing felt is to swing wide the door and invite this one into embodiment."
"Do not speculate as to how you shall express these qualities or claims, for life has a way that you, as man, know not of. Its ways are past finding out. But, I assure you, the day you claim these qualities to the point of conviction, your claims will be honored."
"Take your attention from being imprisoned and begin to feel yourself to be free. FEEL it to the point where it is natural – the very second you do so, those prison bars will dissolve. Apply this same principle to any problem."
"A mood attracts its affinities but it does not create what it attracts."
"When you emerge from prayer you no longer seek, for you have—if you have prayed correctly—subconsciously assumed the reality of the state sought."
"In concentrating on your objective, the secret is to bring it here. You must make elsewhere here and then now imagine that your objective is so close that you can feel it."

In somewhat flowery language, he compared the inner feeling of a completed spiritual claim, to the physical feeling of a completed sexual release. Nothing more is to be done. "When the physical creative act is completed, the sinew which is upon the hollow of man's thigh shrinks, and man finds himself impotent or is halted. In like manner when a man prays successfully he believes that he is already that which he desired to be, therefore he cannot continue desiring to be that which he is already conscious of being. At the moment of satisfaction, physical and psychological, something goes out which in time bears witness to man's creative power."

You're already doing exactly what Neville recommended for almost his entire career.

replies.content:
1) I don't know of any books on that subject. The advice I was given back in the day was to cast readings on any topic that interested me, but not to follow the advice given in those readings when it contradicted basic common sense. That worked fairly well.

comments.title: Plain Division
comments.content:
Mr. Greer and Commentariat,

For what meta-physical reasons might sleep be so evolutionarily conserved? What I mean by this is sleep-like states are present in animals throughout the kingdoms of life. Why? I am familiar with the explanations from biology, which amount to 'it helps regulate metabolic and neurological pathways.' But from a strictly biological standpoint the cost of sleep is extraordinarily high. Daily lowered alertness, movement, and foraging would not at first glance appear to be a successful strategy. So it seems likely to me that the answer is not strictly physical, but rather something about how life, maybe all of creation, interacts with the planes. Any thoughts on this are welcome.

- Wentar Is A Title, Not A Name

replies.title: Re: Plain Division
replies.content:
Quite the contrary, sleep is a very effective evolutionary strategy. Land vertebrates are pretty consistently either diurnal, nocturnal, or crepuscular, because each of these requires radically different sense organs and brain structures. During the times when your senses and your brain aren't adapted to perceive predators and the like, the best thing you can do is huddle down in a sheltered place and remain motionless. Sleep provides for that. Of course once evolution selected for that, animals also picked up the habit of using the sleep period to deal with the aftermath of muscular and neural exertion and get ready for the next day, or night, or twilight period of activity.

Sleep also has important metaphysical dimensions, but the ecological dimension of it also counts!

replies.title: Re: Plain Division
replies.content:
Thank you ever so much for this, and all other responses.
Wentar

comments.title: Reincarnation
comments.content:
If I understand reincarnation correctly, a soul gradually ascends from simple to more complex "beings". I think in the past, you said that one way to think of this is that the more complex the being is, the more potential it has (for both upside and downside).

Within humanity, it seems that - in general - men have more potential (for both upside and downside) than women. Some of this could be cultural. But, when I look throughout history, the biggest achievers and also the worst villains tend to be men. Again, could be cultural.

If it's not cultural and something inherent, would that imply that men are higher up the reincarnation chain than women?

Or is the logic flawed? E.g., measures of achievement and villain-ness are somehow biased. Giving birth is quite an achievement, for instance.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation
replies.content:
In human beings, as in most other mammals (and a lot of birds), females tend to be closer to the average than males. There are plenty of good evolutionary reasons for that; you can sum them up by saying that males are more disposable than females, since the number of females puts a hard limit on the size of the next generation but a very small number of males can fertilize a very large number of females. That's why, in humans and in most other mammals (and a lot of birds), young males go out and try to get themselves killed by taking absurd risks -- they're exposing themselves to Darwinian selection, so the unfit will be weeded out and the fit will then go on to reproduce.

That's the biological matrix we work with. Keep in mind, though, that most souls alternate male and female lives pretty regularly; if you're a man in this incarnation, odds are you were a woman the last time around and will be a woman again next time. So you get to alternate lives when you go as far from the norm as you can manage, with lives where you return to the norm.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation
replies.content: When alternating genders in the course of reincarnation, an eccentric man or a man who has achieved more direct access to the mental plane surely doesn't suddenly lose these character traits in a female incarnation, or did I misunderstand something?

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation
replies.content: Remember that the personality taken on in each incarnation is a reflection of the Individuality, a subset of the soul's potentials suitable for that incarnation. If the soul needs to deal with karma or do something else that requires less eccentricity or less contact with the mental plane, that's how the personality will take shape. That said, there'll still be some eccentricity or some unusual clarity -- it may just be expressed in a less obvious way.

comments.content:
I'm not sure how to phrase this question right but I'll try. I'm really confused about how, for me anyway, kundalini rising/chakra opening are such physical processes, but people say they can eventually lead to enlightenment. On the other hand meditation, which is such a mental and maybe emotional process, can lead to it also. Wouldn't there be people who do lots of meditation but have completely blocked energy channels? And people who get their energy channels opened but don't meditate and so aren't functioning very highly on the mental plane? How do they end up in more or less the same place, or do they?

Also, when bad energy is being moved and cleared out of me, it feels very much like anxiety as it rises. Is that just what negative energy physically feels like, is it my brain trying to interpret the pain and weirdness in a way that's familiar to it, or is it because of the chakra it's been stuck in? replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
First of all, the rising of the kundalini has physical effects but it's not actually a physical process. The energy that rises up the central channel of the spine is spiritual in nature, not physical.

Second, that word "enlightenment" stands for many different kinds of spiritual experiences, some of which correspond to the effects of risen kundalini and some of which don't.

Finally, have you ever gotten water flowing through a pipe that's more than half full of crud? Lots of muck comes gurgling and spraying out of it before the water finally runs clear. That's what happens when the kundalini goes up your spine, too; if anxiety is what comes out, that probably tells you that you had a lot of anxiety clogging up the pipe!

replies.title: Reply
replies.content:
When you say spiritual energy you mean etheric energy right?

I'm going through this K process too and for me it feels like my nerves are on fire on many days (though the intensity rises and falls in cycles).

I've also had changes in emotions (reduction in negative emotions, reduction in the automatic induction of desire, lust etc.), some repairs in my physical body (pain in my stomach etc) and some mental breakthroughs (I see to have realised a bunch of things about meaning, perception, desire etc).

Was it similar for you?

replies.title: Re: Reply
replies.content:
No, I mean spiritual energy as in the energy of the spiritual plane -- the plane above the mental plane, which is above the astral, which is above the etheric. That's why it stirs up so much stuff -- it clears up things on all the planes on which we're manifested.

My kundalini releases so far have been the slow, relatively gentle sort, with lots of popping and cracking in my spine, sudden tension releases, and bursts of insight, but without the rough nervous-system stuff. I'm grateful for that!

replies.title: Re: Reply
replies.content:
You're saying that the spiritual energy is outside our bodies (since we don't have a spiritual body yet) but it clears up the lower plane bodies? Is the celestial fire in plotinus the same thing?

So one thing I did want to ask you is that it seems that this experience is a prelude to the type of enlightenment experience that results in much closer experiences of non-duality/one-ness and a lack of a inner narrative (as some experience it), along with incredible insights/knowledge of things that weren't accessible before, and some weird powers for some people. That would be the same thing as the having your final incarnation before you have a choice to move on?

In my case I can see my intuition getting a lot better, weird help from fate on occasions when i take big risks, and my goal-seeking intensity has reducedl I let things happen and they seem to happen on their own pace, I seem closer to the tao, but it doesn't seem like this might be my final incarnation, unlike for you. I still experience desire (though attenuated and in my control) in dualistic way.(I did have my a vague hint of a past life surface but I'm 31 so maybe more of that stuff comes up over the next few years).

Glad you made through it easy. I'm absolutely getting my ass handed to me on some days.

replies.title: Re: Reply
replies.content: The kundalini is a spiritual-plane force that is connected, through the intervening bodies, with the muladhara chakra (in Western terms, the coccygeal glomus) in the material body. So it's both inside and outside us. Yes, I think that's what Plotinus was talking about. It may or may not result in the transition beyond incarnation, depending on what you do with it! As for the easier way, there are practices to foster that, though I don't think they'll do much once you actually have a release under way.

replies.title: Re: Reply
replies.content:
The idea of Mark Stavish of the secret fire is sort of the subcomponent of it? (basically the etheric energy at the base of the spine rising/transmuting to mental plane energy in the head, very heavy neurological energy in the physical body, all of this is triggered by the external spiritual plane energy?)

One thing I am speculating about is whether this energy activates to different levels/intensities based on a person's spiritual advancement and karma, and that affects where in the incarnation process one is.

replies.title: Re: Reply
replies.content: Yes, the secret fire is the etheric reflection of the primal kundalini energy. As for the relationship between spiritual advancement, karma, and the details of the release, that would make sense, but you'd need to check it experimentally, of course.

replies.title: Re: Reply
replies.content:
Makes sense.

I did have one question for you.

Since you consider this your last incarnation or two, do you experience your normal day to day internal phenomenology as mostly non-dual as described in the eastern traditions (no internal running voice, general happiness, compassion etc) or does the western tradition not really focus on that (ie the ego is developed sufficiently to still experience narrativity / dualism but with understanding and control over it rather than as raw instinctual cause and effect?)

replies.title: Re: Reply
replies.content:
It fascinates me that in the Eastern traditions happiness and compassion are considered nondual, when they're dualistic states -- happiness is the polar opposite of misery, compassion is the polar opposite of selfishness, and you can't have one without the other any more than you can have hot without cold or high without low!

The issue of narrative is more interesting. The Western way is to control that and turn it on and off as needed, rather than simply abolishing it -- the mind in the Western tradition is to be made a responsive tool of the self rather than its enemy. As for emotions, passions, etc., those are also mastered rather than abolished.

comments.title: Planet in Detriment Magic
comments.content: Last week you mentioned one can have "trouble" working magic related to a debilitated planet in one's birthchart. Can you expand on what sort of trouble? How ill advised is this? What about a situation like Venus in detriment (Venus in Scorpio) dignified by triplicity? Thank you for Magic Monday.

replies.title: Re: Planet in Detriment Magic
replies.content: It's specifically a problem in Renaissance astrological magic. Some practitioners have found that if you do this, you don't get the results you wanted, because you activate the debilitated planet and it gives you more of whatever trouble it normally causes you! In your case, you'd want to look at the condition of Venus more generally, what aspects it makes, and also the condition and aspects made by the ruler of the house she's in. If these are more negative than positive, definitely stay away from astrological workings involving Venus.

comments.title: Emanationism and the Chain of Being
comments.content:
I gather that Neoplatonic philosophers like Plotinus and Porphyry may be described as emanationists. My understanding is that in their systems the ineffable One is an impersonal force, that it is the origin of everything and that from it emanate all the gods, angels and lesser spirits and indeed all that exists, in a cascade or chain of being that descends from the highest down to the tiniest grain of dirt.

In his science fiction trilogy beginning with “Out of the Silent Planet,” C.S. Lewis depicts a universe in which the place of the One seems to be taken by Maleldil, viz., by God, such that from Him descend the planetary spirits and all the rest of creation. Similarly, in Tolkien’s legendarium the creator of the universe is Eru Ilúvatar, and a hierarchy of being descends from him through the valar, the maiar and so on down to humble hobbits and their beloved mushrooms. The valar might even be considered as lesser gods below the one god, if that is theologically conceivable.

It appears to me as though the key difference between pagan polytheist emanationism on the one hand and a monotheist hierarchy of being on the other is the nature of the point of origin: is it a personal god, or an impersonal but eternal principle?

Is the difference between these belief systems as I have described it, or am I missing something important?

Personally I find it difficult to figure out the way I think things really are; but maybe this is beyond the capacity of talking monkeys in any case.

replies.title: Re: Emanationism and the Chain of Being
replies.content: You've just identified the main difference between classical Pagan Neoplatonism and Christian Neoplatonism. Christian Neoplatonists identify the One with their god; Pagan Neoplatonists consider this to be wrong, since the One is not a person and has no qualities -- technically speaking it does not even exist, for it is the condition through which existence becomes possible.

replies.title: Re: Emanationism and the Chain of Being
replies.content: It seems to me that a logarithmic spiral is a pretty good graphical representation of the concept of the One, as seen, funnily enough, in the opening credits to certain spy movies. When the image rotates, the spirals seem to converge toward or radiate from a central point; being a dimensionless point it doesn’t exist, yet from it everything emanates.

replies.title: Re: Emanationism and the Chain of Being
replies.content: That's a nice symbolic expression.

replies.title: Re: Emanationism and the Chain of Being
replies.content: I wonder if in this case you might be able to consider the One as the Big Bang itself...

replies.title: Re: Emanationism and the Chain of Being
replies.content: If the Big Bang is more than a modern myth, quite possibly.

replies.title: Re: Emanationism and the Chain of Being
replies.content: "Nature abhors a vacuum", and it would be strange if there was just this one universe that started with just this one big bang with just this one set of details. I would think the big bang would be an interesting symbol, but like in Atenism with the sun disc, only a symbol.

replies.title: Re: Emanationism and the Chain of Being
replies.content: And a hero who embodies the rigorous endurance of sefirah 007...

replies.title: Re: Emanationism and the Chain of Being
replies.content:
That would be John Dee, of course!

(007 was Dee's code number in Elizabeth I's intelligence service. I have no idea if that's where Ian Fleming got the idea, but it's certainly possible.)

comments.title: Caucasian Yoga
comments.content:
Mr. Greer, thank you for opening your forum up to questions today. I've been wanting to ask you about this, but I didn't know how and now here is this opportunity. I see that recently you wrote a book about the Five Tibetan Rites. I practice Qigong and in my search for routines I found the 5 Rites and practiced them for a while achieving 21 reps on each set. It seems like an eclectic system that provides exercises that are similar to Qigong, but also descends from Zoroastrianism and maybe some other things... Murat Yagan seems to have picked it up and extended it into his system of Ahmsta Kebzeh. Books written by Yagan seem to be out of print or very hard to find. The most popular text on the subject is a book written by Stefan Colonna Walewski titled, A System of Caucasian Yoga. The main sticking point for me is that this book is in the form of very bad copies of a hand-written manuscript. If you are familiar with the system you probably already know all of this. So, my question is, are you familiar with the system? If so, what is your opinion of it? And, would you ever consider re-writing Walewski's manuscript in a new book that could also provide more details about the tradition that it originates from?

P.S. In terms of Western systems, I've tried some of the Golden Dawn exercises and they just don't feel right for me. I've been eying up your Golden Section material and am reviewing the Hermetic Rose documents, also. I have a strong attraction to Rosicrucianism and recently joined B.O.T.A. and am working through the Tarot lessons. I've been in search for a system that builds up my energy like Qigong, but also has teachings that help me function more effectively/intuitively in the world more than when I'm just doing the physical exercises. Thanks once again,
Scott

comments.title: Polytheism
comments.content:
Greetings, JMG,

I heard your interview with Rhyd Wildermuth on polytheism. I've had experiences with deities of different pantheons at different times, such as Ganesh and Shiva from Hinduism, Athena and Hekate from Greece, etc. I'm wondering your perspective on whether it's ok to be devoted to a variety of gods from different pantheons, or whether it's better to commit to a specific tradition such as Hinduism or Hellenismos. Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Polytheism
replies.content: That varies from person to person. Certainly in ancient times you routinely saw people worshipping deities from a wide range of pantheons; Epona, the Celtic horse goddess, became the patron goddess of the entire Roman cavalry corps, worshipped by cavalrymen from North Africa, the Balkans, and everywhere else. But it really does depend on which gods call to you.
